does: This paper presents a comprehensive empirical evaluation of data leakage risks in tool-using language model agents across realistic, non-adversarial operational scenarios. The authors from Singapore and Korea AI Safety Institutes jointly assessed five LLM-based agents on 12 realistic tasks spanning customer support, DevOps, web automation, and enterprise productivity. Rather than focusing on adversarial attacks, the evaluation identifies leakage risks arising from benign user requests, examining five specific risk categories: lack of data awareness (failing to recognize sensitive information), audience awareness (misunderstanding who should access data), policy compliance (violating organizational policies), data minimization (unnecessary retention or exposure), and access-boundary awareness (violating permission boundaries). The evaluation used independent testing environments, task-specific LLM-judge rubrics, and found that none of the tested agents achieved adequate performance across these dimensions.
connects: This work builds on growing concern about data security and privacy in AI agent systems, extending beyond the established literature on adversarial prompt injection and jailbreak attacks that has dominated agent safety research. It responds to the gap between security-focused evaluations and realistic deployment scenarios where agents encounter sensitive enterprise data daily. The paper connects to broader research on AI system trustworthiness, data privacy principles in machine learning, and responsible AI deployment frameworks that emphasize non-adversarial failure modes. It also relates to work on AI alignment and behavioral evaluation methodologies, drawing on LLM-as-judge approaches for assessing complex agent behaviors. The multi-institute collaboration reflects emerging standardization efforts in AI safety evaluation, building on prior work establishing evaluation frameworks for AI system risks in organizational contexts.
enables: This evaluation establishes baseline measurements and risk categories that future agent development can improve upon, enabling more privacy-preserving tool-use architectures. The findings motivate research into technical solutions such as data-aware prompt design, fine-tuning agents to respect organizational policies, implementing access control mechanisms, and developing information minimization strategies in agent behavior. The work sets up opportunities for building practical deployment guidelines and safety standards for enterprise AI agents. It enables follow-up research on developing evaluation methods that scale across diverse organizational contexts and agent types. The identified risk categories provide a framework for designing agent architectures with built-in privacy and compliance safeguards, and support development of testing methodologies for regulatory compliance of AI systems handling sensitive data in real-world settings.
